risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomised controlled trial" and "Exclusionary criteria and allocation procedures have previously been described [5]." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated in this report. Without an explicit method such as a random number table, computer-generated sequence, or similar, the random sequence generation domain cannot be judged low risk from this text alone.

Summary: The provided text describes a randomized, parallel four-arm trial and says that "Exclusionary criteria and allocation procedures have previously been described," but it does not explicitly state how allocation was concealed from the recruiters/enrollers. There is no description of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Randomization is mentioned, but concealment is not; therefore the allocation concealment risk remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The interventions were clearly different and delivered in the trial as psychotherapy/exercise versus specialist medical care, with no statement that participants or treating personnel were blinded. The report describes distinct therapy manuals and delivery by different specialists ("CBT, delivered by clinical psychologists or nurse therapists"; "GET, delivered by physiotherapists"; "SMC was provided by CFS doctors"), implying allocation would be known to those involved. Because the main outcomes include self-reported quality of life, fatigue, disability, and service use, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence them. Comment: This represents a dominant high risk of performance bias across study outcomes.

Summary: The main outcomes were patient-reported questionnaire measures: "Quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) were generated from the EQ-5D health-related quality of life questionnaire" and the trial also used "the Chalder fatigue questionnaire" and "the Short Form-36 physical function sub-scale". The methods do not report that outcome assessors were blinded. Because the patients themselves provided these subjective outcomes, and participant blinding is not described here, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement/detection bias.

Quote: "641 patients were recruited, one of whom withdrew consent." Comment: Attrition appears low overall, and the paper reports that costs and QALYs were available for 570 (89%) participants (ranging from 85% GET to 93% SMC), with no indication that missingness was markedly imbalanced or related to outcome; the authors also note that those with complete data differed only in ethnicity and not other baseline characteristics. This suggests incomplete outcome data is unlikely to have biased the results materially.
